meis.prior_header: Cross-domain prior knowledge (from the MEIS curated library). Treat these relations as well-established background facts you can use to structure your hypotheses, choose informative observations, and explain the system to others. You do not need to rediscover them from data:

explanations:
<thought>The age-to-length relationship is not something a beginner should assume is linear or smoothly increasing. The safest approach is to think of it as an unknown curve over ages 0 to 5 that may rise, fall, and change steeply in some regions. To predict length from age, use a flexible interpolation mindset: estimate values from nearby ages rather than extending a straight-line trend across the whole range. Give extra attention to local behavior, because the curve can change direction and may have narrow peaks or dips. Predictions are most reliable when the target age lies between several nearby measured ages; they are less reliable near boundaries or in gaps with sparse information. If forced to predict from an explanation alone, imagine a wavy, non-monotonic curve and avoid assuming “older means longer.” A practical rule is: find the closest surrounding ages, compare their lengths, and infer the target from the local pattern—whether it appears rising, falling, or turning. In short, use local interpolation on a potentially irregular curve, not a single global trend.</thought>
<answer>Use local, flexible interpolation on a possibly wavy age-length curve over ages 0 to 5; do not assume the relationship is linear, smooth everywhere, or always increasing with age.</answer>

scientist_messages:
role:system, messaage:Sea cows are different lengths at different ages.
Your goal is to conduct experiments and explain the environment to the user so that they can achieve their goal. The goal of the user is to be able to predict the length of a sea cow given its age.
Make observations by specifying a single age you want to observe with a real number.
The environment will return the length of a dugong at that age.
The age values are between 0 and 5.
You may also think before providing your predictions.

Here is an example:
<thought> your thought </thought>
<observe>2</observe>
When asked to answer a question about the environment, respond in the format specified in the question.
<thought> your thought </thought>
<answer> your answer </answer>

role:user, messaage:Think about where to observe next. Articulate your strategy for choosing measurements in <thought>.
Provide a new measurement point in the format:
<thought>your thought</thought>
<observe> your observation</observe>
Make an observation now.

role:assistant, messaage:<thought>I’ll start near the middle of the age range to get a baseline length and help infer the overall growth pattern before choosing edge or intermediate ages.</thought>
<observe>2.5</observe>

This will help determine whether a simple linear model is adequate or whether growth accelerates or decelerates with age.</thought>

Measuring at 1.5 will help determine whether the curve is smooth and possibly peaked near the center, which is useful for fitting a quadratic-like relationship.</thought>

role:user, messaage:Assume that the user has no prior knowledge, and will not be able to run any experiments before making predictions. 
They will make predictions based solely on your explanation, so provide as much detail as possible. You CANNOT provide your own experiments or observations.
Limit your explanation to 200 words.

role:assistant, messaage:<thought>The age-to-length relationship is not something a beginner should assume is linear or smoothly increasing. The safest approach is to think of it as an unknown curve over ages 0 to 5 that may rise, fall, and change steeply in some regions. To predict length from age, use a flexible interpolation mindset: estimate values from nearby ages rather than extending a straight-line trend across the whole range. Give extra attention to local behavior, because the curve can change direction and may have narrow peaks or dips. Predictions are most reliable when the target age lies between several nearby measured ages; they are less reliable near boundaries or in gaps with sparse information. If forced to predict from an explanation alone, imagine a wavy, non-monotonic curve and avoid assuming “older means longer.” A practical rule is: find the closest surrounding ages, compare their lengths, and infer the target from the local pattern—whether it appears rising, falling, or turning. In short, use local interpolation on a potentially irregular curve, not a single global trend.</thought>
<answer>Use local, flexible interpolation on a possibly wavy age-length curve over ages 0 to 5; do not assume the relationship is linear, smooth everywhere, or always increasing with age.</answer>
